nativeRange.habitat: Slow-moving and still waters of the upper Río Orinoco basin including periodic wetlands, large floodplains, lagoons, inundated forest, and tributary streams; bottom-dwelling among leaf litter and dense vegetation with minimal water flow

decorPreferences: fine sand substrate to protect delicate barbels
dense planting (Java fern, Anubias, Cryptocoryne, floating plants)
driftwood and leaf litter for shelter and foraging
shady hiding spots under broad-leaved plants
gentle filtration with minimal water movement

diet.notes: Sinking micro foods are essential — nano pellets, sinking wafers, micro granules, and crushed flake. Relishes small live and frozen meaty foods including baby brine shrimp, daphnia, micro worms, and small bloodworm. Unlike larger corydoras, habrosus corys show a preference for meaty foods over algae or vegetable matter. Must be directly fed; cannot subsist on leftovers alone.

goodWith: dwarf shrimp (adults — fry may be eaten)
other dwarf corydoras (Gastrodermus pygmaeus, Gastrodermus hastatus)
avoidWith: any fish large enough to swallow them
fast, competitive feeders that prevent them reaching sinking foods

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species image for Hoplisoma habrosum (formerly Corydoras habrosus). Verify license before uploading to R2.

summary: The Salt-and-Pepper Pygmy Corydoras (Hoplisoma habrosum) is the largest of the three dwarf cory species at ~3.5 cm, distinguished from the mid-water Gastrodermus pygmaeus and G. hastatus by its strictly bottom-dwelling behaviour. Reclassified from Corydoras habrosus in 2024. Easy to keep in groups of 8+ in a soft-water planted nano tank.
careNotes: Hoplisoma habrosum (reclassified from Corydoras habrosus in the 2024 Dias/Britto revision that also moved G. pygmaeus and G. hastatus out of Corydoras) is the largest of the three dwarf corydoras species, with round-bellied females reaching ~3.5 cm and slimmer males slightly smaller. Unlike Gastrodermus pygmaeus and G. hastatus, which routinely hover in the mid-water column, habrosus is a true bottom-dweller that forages through the substrate using its sensitive barbels — a key identification and husbandry point. A fine sand substrate (minimum 3 cm deep) is essential to protect those barbels; coarser gravel will cause erosion and barbel loss, leading to secondary infections. Keep in groups of at least 8–10 individuals; groups of 10–15+ produce natural, confident shoaling behaviour. A 40 L (approximately 10 US gal) tank with a 60 cm footprint is a workable minimum for a single-species group, though larger setups allow for better tankmate options. Soft, slightly acidic water (pH 5.5–7.5, gH 2–12) replicates their upper Orinoco floodplain habitat; hard alkaline water stresses them and shortens lifespan. Water temperature 20–26°C — the lower end of this range can trigger spawning behaviour. Keep nitrates low through regular partial water changes; these small fish are sensitive to accumulated waste. Gentle filtration (sponge filters are ideal) maintains flow without sweeping them off the substrate. Like all Callichthyidae, they are facultative air breathers and will periodically dash to the surface for a gulp of air — this is normal behaviour, but persistent surface gulping signals low dissolved oxygen. Handle with care: the pectoral and dorsal fin spines carry mild venom capable of causing a painful sting and localised swelling if the fish is gripped barehanded. MERGE CANDIDATE: fw-fish-191 ("Habrosus Cory", filed as Corydoras habrosus) represents the same species under the pre-2024 name and should be merged into this entry.
breedingNotes: Breeding follows the typical Corydoras pattern and is achievable in the home aquarium. Condition the group with generous feeding of live and frozen foods (baby brine shrimp, micro worms). Trigger spawning with large (30–50%) partial water changes using slightly cooler, soft water to simulate seasonal rains. Males pursue and embrace females in the characteristic T-position; the female deposits small batches of adhesive eggs (~1–4 per clutch, many clutches per session) on glass, broad plant leaves, or decor. Incubation is 3–4 days at 24°C. Remove eggs or adults after spawning to prevent predation; eggs are prone to fungus — gentle water flow or antifungal treatment (methylene blue) improves hatch rates. Newly hatched fry are tiny and require infusoria or commercial fry foods for the first week before graduating to micro worms and baby brine shrimp.

primary.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Care Guide for Habrosus Corydoras
primary.notes: Primary trade/hobbyist source. Provided adult size (females to 3.5 cm), temperature 20–26°C, pH 6–8, soft to moderately hard water, minimum 10-gal tank, bottom swim zone, diet (sinking meaty foods), grouping (8–12+), breeding triggers, and compatible tankmates. Notes habrosus as the bottom-dwelling dwarf cory distinct from mid-water pygmaeus/hastatus.

additional.name: SeriouslyFish — Corydoras habrosus
additional.notes: Scientific hobbyist reference. Provided adult size 30–35 mm SL, temperature 20–26°C, pH 5.5–7.5, hardness 36–179 ppm (~2–10 dGH), native range (upper Río Orinoco, eastern Colombia and western Venezuela, restricted to left-bank tributaries), habitat (lagoons, inundated forest and grassland, minimal flow), minimum tank dimensions (60 × 30 cm), grouping (at least 4–6, ideally 10+), diet (sinking dried foods plus small live/frozen), breeding (eggs on glass/vegetation, incubation 3–4 days), and sexual dimorphism (females broader and deeper-bodied).

additional.name: FishBase — Hoplisoma habrosum (Corydoras habrosus)
additional.notes: Taxonomic and range reference. Lists current accepted name as Hoplisoma habrosum (Weitzman, 1960); family Callichthyidae; order Siluriformes; pH 6.0–8.0; native to upper Orinoco, South America. FishBase max length of 2.0 cm SL (male/unsexed) is conservative; SeriouslyFish and Aquarium Co-Op both confirm females reach ~3.5 cm TL. Used FishBase for taxonomy confirmation and pH cross-check.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping (Fishkeeper.co.uk) — Salt & Pepper Cory
additional.notes: UK trade reference. Provided adult size 4 cm, temperature 22–26°C, pH 6.0–7.2, hardness up to 12 dH, sandy substrate requirement, grouping recommendation (minimum 10), breeding (large cooler water changes, adhesive eggs, hatch in 3–6 days, fungus prone), diet (catfish pellets, granules, flake, frozen foods including bloodworm and brineshrimp), and notes on recent split from Corydoras into Hoplisoma.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Salt and Pepper Catfish
additional.notes: Cross-reference for reclassification, native range, and conservation status. Confirms current name Hoplisoma habrosum (formerly Corydoras habrosus), native to upper Orinoco in Venezuela and Colombia, habitat in periodic wetlands and large floodplains with leaf litter, Near Threatened (IUCN 3.1), and notes facultative air-breathing behaviour.

fish.breedingNotes: Achievable in a dedicated breeding setup with soft, slightly acidic water. Condition with live/frozen foods; trigger spawning with large partial water changes using cooler soft water. Eggs adhesive, deposited in small batches on glass and plant leaves; incubation 3–4 days. Fry very small — require infusoria initially. Eggs prone to fungus; antifungal treatment improves hatch rate.
